goals.description: Contributors and consumers have an accepted architectural charter, explicit governance and compatibility rules, and a reproducible multi-module workspace with enforceable target and quality policies.

requirements.description: A contributor can read an accepted foundation RFC that defines MNF's vision, principles, terminology, architectural layers, dependency direction, and v0.1 boundaries.
A contributor can follow a documented RFC lifecycle with named statuses, acceptance authority, review expectations, and rules for breaking architectural changes.
A package consumer can distinguish experimental, candidate, and stable public APIs and understand the compatibility promise of each status.
A contributor can identify the chosen project license, fixture licensing rules, mooncakes.io owner/namespace, and module naming policy before public release.
A developer can clone the repository and operate mb-core, mb-color, and mb-image as three independently publishable MoonBit modules in one workspace.
A developer can reproduce the v0.1 development environment from a checked-in toolchain policy that records the exact moon, moonc, and moonrun baseline.
A consumer can inspect every public package's explicit supported-target declaration and determine whether it supports native, wasm, wasm-gc, or js.
A maintainer can run root-level format, check, test, documentation, package-content, and dependency-DAG validation without manually entering each module.
A maintainer can verify portable package behavior on every declared target while LLVM remains clearly non-blocking and experimental.

decisions.description: RFC 0001 is the architectural charter and must define vision, terminology, layer boundaries, dependency direction, portability policy, v0.1 scope, and governance.
Use the lifecycle Draft -> Proposed -> Accepted -> Implemented, with Rejected and Superseded terminal states; every transition is recorded in the RFC header and repository history.

Acceptance requires one complete evidenced route with no unresolved blocker: two maintainers, a seven-day project-lead review while fewer than two maintainers exist, or sole-project-owner-bootstrap while the canonical roster contains exactly one unique maintainer who is the project owner; the sole-owner route requires both mandatory edge reviews and expires when another maintainer is added.
New modules, public dependency-direction changes, and breaking architectural changes require an accepted RFC. Implementation PRs may not silently redefine an accepted boundary.
License project-authored source, documentation, and generated fixtures under Apache-2.0 to provide a permissive license with an explicit patent grant.

Maintain a fixture manifest recording source, author, retrieval date, SHA-256, SPDX/license, redistribution status, and expected use. Prefer generated fixtures; do not commit externally sourced fixtures without confirmed redistribution permission.

Reserve moonbit-foundation as the intended mooncakes.io owner namespace and use moonbit-foundation/mb-core, moonbit-foundation/mb-color, and moonbit-foundation/mb-image. Public publication is blocked until ownership is verified; local manifests still use these final intended names to avoid a later rename.
Keep module names domain-specific and independently publishable. Do not add an umbrella mnf/all module or package, and do not force lockstep versions.

Public APIs are labeled experimental, candidate, or stable. Experimental APIs have no compatibility promise; candidate APIs require documented migration notes for changes; stable APIs follow Semantic Versioning.
v0.1 packages begin as candidate unless explicitly marked experimental. No package is called stable before its contract, conformance evidence, and release policy meet the stable gate.

Stable breaking changes require an accepted RFC, a major-version change, a migration guide, and compatibility qualification of direct dependants. Stable removals require at least one prior minor release of deprecation unless a documented security exception applies.
Stability status and supported targets must be visible in package documentation and checked metadata, not inferred from repository location or version number alone.
Use one moon.work with three member modules under modules/mb-core, modules/mb-color, and modules/mb-image; each module has its own manifest, version, changelog, and publication lifecycle.
Pin the v0.1 development baseline exactly to moon 0.1.20260713 (75c7e1f), recording the bundled moonc v0.10.4+2cc641edf and moonrun 0.1.20260713 in checked-in policy and CI logs.

Use moon.mod.json for the v0.1 compatibility floor while moon.mod rollout remains transitional. Every public package declares +js+wasm+wasm-gc+native; native-only leaf adapters declare native. Avoid a restrictive module-level target intersection.

Provide one root PowerShell 7 quality entry point that runs formatting checks, workspace checks, tests, documentation checks, package-content inspection, explicit target-matrix checks, and dependency-DAG/metadata validation. CI invokes the same entry point and treats LLVM as experimental and non-blocking.

research_items.description: policy/foundation.json owns values that automation compares: module names, paths, versions, public package paths, stability, required targets, allowed dependency edges, tool versions, and publication.blocked=true with the namespace-ownership reason. Prose documents link to and explain those values; quality validation rejects drift and separate JSON owners for the same facts.

moon.work lists exactly the three locked member paths. Each member uses its final intended registry name, own version, changelog and README, and normal module dependencies resolved locally by the workspace; per-module packaging remains independent and the validator rejects self-edges, unknown modules, cycles, and reverse edges.

Give each root package a package-private scaffold definition and basic test but no public domain API. moon info should expose an empty or intentionally minimal interface while format, check, test, docs, and package commands exercise a real package, avoiding placeholder compatibility debt.

scripts/quality.ps1 accepts Required or LlvmExperimental. Required validates PowerShell and exact tools, policy and metadata, formatting, explicit js/wasm/wasm-gc/native checks and tests, docs/info, and package contents in fixed order; LLVM runs separately and non-blocking.
Put module usage and status documentation in .mbt.md so Moon checks mbt check fences through moon check and runs document tests through moon test; run moon doc --frozen as a separate generation gate.
Do not create a second RFC 0001; update the existing Draft in place and preserve history.
Do not leave resolved questions in the accepted RFC; namespace, module count and names, license, acceptance authority, and compatibility labels are locked.
Do not track latest toolchains or movable action tags; exact reproducibility requires the action commit, exact toolchain input, and post-install identity checks.
Do not use path dependencies for workspace members; declare normal module dependencies and let moon.work resolve local members.
Do not rely only on --target all logs; explicitly loop required targets so evidence identifies the failing backend and policy is compared target by target.
Do not treat module-level target metadata as sufficient; every public package must carry explicit support metadata.
Do not run moon work sync inside the quality gate because it mutates manifests and can hide drift; synchronize during scaffolding or release preparation and validate with frozen state.
Do not publish to test the scaffold; packaging is permitted but publication remains blocked until namespace ownership is verified.
Do not expose placeholder public APIs; use a real package with no fake domain contract.
Do not build custom workspace orchestration that enters modules for check, test, or info; use moon.work and root Moon commands because workspace context is built into Moon.
Do not build a regex parser for moon.mod.json or policy; use PowerShell ConvertFrom-Json to avoid quoting and ordering errors.
Do not assume what target all means; use an explicit required target array plus Moon commands because all currently excludes LLVM and may evolve.
Do not build a separate Markdown code-fence runner; use Moon .mbt.md and document tests integrated with moon check and moon test.
Do not use a home-grown file walker as the package source of truth; use moon package --frozen --list because it reports Moon's actual publication selection.
Do not create custom version grammar; use SemVer-compliant module versions and documented policy because Mooncakes publication requires SemVer.
Do not invent license labels; use the SPDX identifier Apache-2.0 because Moon module metadata expects an SPDX value.

RFC 0001 may contain correct prose but lack authentic route evidence or retain blocking questions. Record explicit transitions and route-specific evidence; never fabricate the seven-day project-lead interval, and consume the sole owner's existing conditional preauthorization only after both mandatory edge reviews pass.

Earlier research may use mnf-core examples or shorter filesystem names. Treat CONTEXT.md as authoritative and assert the exact moonbit-foundation/mb-* identities and modules/mb-* paths in foundation.json.
CI may install moon but silently receive a different moonc or moonrun build. Compare normalized output from all three commands before any build and print all identities to CI logs.

Omitting supported-targets claims all backends, while a restrictive module declaration can silently narrow package declarations. Keep the module and each Phase 1 public package on the full portable set, reserve native for leaf adapters, and validate normalized sets rather than string order.
Formatting or dependency synchronization can repair files in CI and hide drift. Use moon fmt --check, frozen commands, non-mutating metadata validation, and a clean tracked-diff assertion.

A module package may include root fixtures, other modules, generated _build content, or credentials. Run moon -C <module> package --frozen --list for each member and compare the result with allowed publication patterns.

An experimental LLVM failure may block Phase 1 or be advertised as support after a shallow check. Keep LLVM out of required metadata, run it in an isolated continue-on-error job, and label the result experimental and non-blocking.

A movable setup action ref can execute changed code or a pull request may receive publication credentials. Pin the action to the researched full SHA, grant read-only workflow permissions, configure no Mooncakes token in Phase 1, and retain an independent version gate.

edge_items.description: Review the compatibility promise at each adjacent stability transition: experimental to candidate and candidate to stable.
Reject an empty or missing stability label, migration note, stable-gate evidence, or checked metadata value.
Enforce ordered promotion and removal rules so stable cannot precede qualification and removal cannot precede required deprecation except for a documented security exception.
Compare each public package target declaration with its adjacent module and canonical policy declarations so intersections cannot silently narrow support.
Reject an absent or empty public-package supported-target declaration rather than interpreting omission as all backends.
Compare target declarations as exact normalized sets so source ordering neither creates false drift nor conceals a missing or extra target.
Verify each adjacent quality stage hands a validated checkout to the next stage: toolchain, policy, format, target checks and tests, docs/info, then package inspection.
Reject an empty workspace member set, package inventory, target loop, validation stage, or package-content result.
Enforce the fixed non-mutating quality-stage order so version and policy drift fails before build work and package inspection follows successful qualification.
Run check and test as an adjacent pair for every declared required target so no backend receives only partial evidence.
Reject an empty required-target set or a required target with no check and test evidence.
Keep LLVM after and outside the required target lane so experimental results cannot mask or block required support.
Manually review the accepted charter for an omitted architectural boundary case.
Manually review lifecycle and acceptance authority for an omitted transition or authority case.
Manually review licensing, namespace, and naming policy for an omitted pre-publication case.
Manually review independent package and publication behavior from each member after scaffolding.
Manually compare normalized output from all three binaries with policy and CI logs on the pinned runner.

prohibitions.description: MUST NOT mark an RFC Accepted without authentic authority, approval or elapsed-review evidence, and objection disposition.
MUST NOT call an API stable before its contract, conformance evidence, and release-policy gate pass.
MUST NOT commit an external fixture without confirmed redistribution and complete provenance.
MUST NOT publish under the intended namespace while ownership is unverified.
MUST NOT advertise LLVM as required support or allow its experimental result to block or mask the required target lane.
